---
title: "Spatial metabolomics workflow using the cherry_tomato_msi example dataset"
author: "Xinyin Deng"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Spatial metabolomics workflow using the cherry_tomato_msi example dataset}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  \usepackage[UTF-8]{inputenc}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  fig.align = "center"
)

knitr::include_graphics(system.file("extdata", "Cherry_tomato.png", package = "spatMetaCluster"))

```


# Overview
This tutorial demonstrates a minimal spatial metabolomics workflow using the
cherry_tomato_msi example dataset included in spatMetaCluster.

cherry_tomato_msi is a toy MSImagingExperiment object designed for package
examples and tutorials. It is not a real MSI acquisition dataset. The
spatial structure and RGB information were generated from a real cherry tomato
image, while the m/z features were manually defined for demonstration purposes.
Therefore, the m/z values in this dataset do not carry biological or chemical
interpretation.

Despite its simplified nature, the dataset is useful for illustrating:

MSI object inspection
spectral visualization
ion image visualization
dimension reduction
spatial clustering
clustering with spatMetaCluster

For reference tutorials on the Cardinal package, please see[Cardinal vignettes](https://github.com/kuwisdelu/CardinalWorkflows/tree/devel/vignettes)


![cherry tomato](~/spatMetaCluster/inst/extdata/Cherry_tomato.png)


# Load packages and example data
```{r Load-MSI-data,echo=TRUE}
library(Cardinal)
library(ggplot2)
library(spatMetaCluster)

data(cherry_tomato_msi)
tomato <- cherry_tomato_msi
tomato
```

The example dataset contains 20 m/z features and 43,253 spectra. It is
intentionally lightweight so that examples remain fast and reproducible.

# Inspect the dataset structure
We first inspect the overall structure of the MSI object.
```{r MSI-data,echo=TRUE}
tomato
```

This object is a centroided MSImagingExperiment with:

20 features
43,253 spectra
spatial coordinates (x, y)
RGB pixel metadata
one intensity assay
The m/z range spans from 141.1193 to 457.5927.

# Visualize a representative spectrum

A useful first step is to examine the spectrum from one pixel. Below we plot
the spectrum for pixel index 1020 and annotate several peaks.

```{r MSI-mean,echo=TRUE,fig.height=6,fig.width=12}
tomato <- summarizeFeatures(tomato, c(Mean="mean"))

Cardinal::plot(
  tomato,
  #i = 1020,
  isPeaks = TRUE,
  annPeaks = 6,
  xlim=c(140,160),
  "Mean", xlab="m/z", ylab="Intensity"
)
```

Because this is a toy dataset with manually defined m/z values, the peak pattern
should be interpreted only as a visualization example.

# Visualize ion images

We can display ion images for all available m/z features.

```{r MSI-TIC,echo=TRUE,fig.height=6,fig.width=8}
tomato <- summarizePixels(tomato, c(TIC="sum"))

Cardinal::image(tomato, "TIC")

image(
  tomato,
  mz = featureData(tomato)$mz,
  smooth = "gaussian",
  enhance = "hist",
  scale = TRUE,
  key = T
)
```

This panel provides a quick view of spatial intensity variation across the
cherry tomato tissue. Since the spatial pattern was generated from an image-based
template, the resulting ion maps are suitable for demonstrating image-style MSI
visualization workflows.

# Dimension reduction with PCA

Principal component analysis (PCA) provides a low-dimensional summary of the
spectral variation across pixels.

```{r PCA,echo=TRUE}
tomato_pca <- Cardinal::PCA(tomato, ncomp = 4)
tomato_pca
```

We can visualize the PCA components as spatial images.
```{r PCA-plot,echo=TRUE,fig.height=4,fig.width=6}
image(
  tomato_pca,
  smooth = "adaptive",
  enhance = "histogram"
)
```

PCA is useful for identifying dominant global patterns in the dataset, although
in this toy example the components mainly reflect the manually constructed
structure in the data.

# Dimension reduction with NMF

Non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) is another commonly used decomposition
method for MSI data.

```{r NMF,echo=TRUE}
tomato_nmf <- Cardinal::NMF(tomato, ncomp = 4, niter = 30)
tomato_nmf
```

We again visualize the resulting components.
```{r NMF-plot,echo=TRUE,fig.height=4,fig.width=6}
image(
  tomato_nmf,
  smooth = "adaptive",
  enhance = "histogram"
)
```

NMF often produces parts-based spatial patterns, which can be easier to interpret
in imaging data.

# Spatial clustering with spatial shrunken centroids

Cardinal provides the spatialShrunkenCentroids() method for spatially aware
clustering of MSI data.

```{r spatialShrunkenCentroids,echo=TRUE}
set.seed(2026)
tomato_ssc <- Cardinal::spatialShrunkenCentroids(
  tomato,
  weights = "adaptive",
  r = 2,
  k = 4,
  s = 2
)

tomato_ssc
```

We can display the resulting segmentation map.
```{r spatialShrunkenCentroids-plot,echo=TRUE,fig.height=4,fig.width=6}
image(tomato_ssc, i = 1)
```

This gives a spatially smoothed cluster assignment across the tissue region.

# Clustering with spatMetaCluster

Next, we demonstrate the clustering workflow implemented in spatMetaCluster.

Because this step depends on an external Python environment, the code is shown
but not evaluated automatically in the vignette.

The Python backend was tested with a conda environment containing Python 3.9, `numpy` 1.24.4, and `umap-learn` 0.5.7. Other compatible versions may also work when properly configured through `reticulate`.


```{r spatMetaCluster,echo=TRUE}
res <- spatMetaCluster::spatial_kmeans_workflow(
  msi_obj = tomato,
  python_path = "/home/hsinyinteng/miniconda3/envs/dxy_python9/bin/python",
  centers = 4,
  metric = "cosine",
  n_neighbors = 25L,
  min_dist = 0.3,
  n_components = 2L
)

head(res$cluster_df)
```

In this example, the workflow uses:
centers = 4 clusters
cosine distance
UMAP neighborhood size of 25
min_dist = 0.3
a 2-dimensional embedding

# Plot the clustering result
The output cluster assignments can be visualized using ggplot2.

```{r spatMetaCluster-plot,echo=TRUE,fig.height=4,fig.width=6}
cluster_df <- res$cluster_df

ggplot(cluster_df, aes(x, y, color = factor(kmeans_cluster))) +
  geom_point(size = 0.1) +
  scale_y_reverse() +
  coord_fixed() +
  theme_void() +
  labs(color = "Cluster") +
  guides(
    color = guide_legend(
      override.aes = list(size = 4)
    )
  )
```
This plot provides a simple spatial view of the cluster labels produced by the
workflow.

# Notes on interpretation
The cherry_tomato_msi dataset is intentionally lightweight and simplified. It
is designed to make package examples fast, stable, and easy to understand.

A few caveats should be kept in mind:

The dataset contains only 20 m/z features.
The m/z values are manually defined and have no real biochemical meaning.
The spatial patterns are image-derived rather than experimentally measured.
Results are appropriate for workflow demonstration, but not for biological
inference or method benchmarking.


# Summary
Using cherry_tomato_msi, we demonstrated how to:

1.load an example MSImagingExperiment
2.inspect spectra and ion images
3.perform PCA and NMF
4.run spatial shrunken centroids clustering
5.apply the spatMetaCluster clustering workflow

This toy dataset provides a compact and reproducible way to explore the core functions of spatMetaCluster.


# Acknowledgement
This package is designed to work with `Cardinal` MSI objects, and some
demonstration steps in this vignette follow a workflow style similar to the
Cardinal tutorials. The included `cherry_tomato_msi` dataset is a manually
constructed toy example created for demonstration purposes and is not derived
from the example datasets distributed with Cardinal.


# SessionInfo
```{r SessionInfo,echo=TRUE}
sessionInfo()
```
